audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman phoning a box office to ask about a festival in the town of kenton.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Kenton Festival box office.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'm coming to Kenton for a few days' holiday next month.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And a friend told me there's a festival.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: She gave me this number to find out about it.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The festival begins on the 16th of May, and goes on till the 19th.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that's great.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'll be there from the 15th till the 19th.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The festival starts on the 16th of May, so 16th has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Kenton Festival box office.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'm coming to Kenton for a few days' holiday next month, and a friend told me there's a festival.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: She gave me this number to find out about it.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's right, the festival begins on the 16th of May, and goes on till the 19th.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that's great.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'll be there from the 15th till the 19th.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So, could you tell me the program, please?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, on the first day, there's the opening ceremony in the town center.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: People start gathering around 2 o'clock, to get a good place to see from, and the events will start at 2.45, and finish about 5.30.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'll make sure I get there early to get a good spot.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The festival will be officially opened by the mayor.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: He'll just speak for a few minutes, welcoming everyone to the festival.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: All the town councillors will be there, and of course lots of other people.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Then there'll be a performance by a band.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Most years we have a children's choir, but this year the local army cadets offered to perform, and they're very good.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh-huh.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: After that, a community group from the town will perform a play they've written themselves, just a short one.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's about Helen Tungate.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I don't know if you've heard of her.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I certainly have.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: She was a scientist years ago.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: She was born in Kenton exactly 100 years ago, so we're celebrating her centenary.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm a biologist, so I've always been interested in her.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I didn't realize she came from Kenton.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, all that will take place in the afternoon, and later as the sun sets, there'll be a firework display.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You should go to the park to watch, as you'll get the best view from there.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And the display takes place on the opposite side of the river.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's always one of the most popular events in the festival.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Sounds great.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And what's happening on the other days?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: There are several events that go on the whole time.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: For example, the students of the art college have produced a number of videos, all connected with relationships between children and their grandparents.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That sounds interesting.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It makes a change from children and parents, doesn't it?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Ha, exactly, uh, because the art college is in use for classes throughout the festival.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The videos are being shown in Handsworth House.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: How do you spell the name?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: HANDSWORTH.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Handsworth House, it's close to the Town Hall.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Now let me see, what else can I tell you about?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Are there any displays of ballet dancing?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'm particularly interested in that, as I do it as a hobby.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: There isn't any ballet, I'm afraid, but there'll be a demonstration of traditional dances from all round the country.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that would be nice.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Where is that being held?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's in the market in the town center, the outdoor one, not the covered market.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And it's on at 2 and 5 every afternoon of the festival, apart from the first day.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Lovely.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'm interested in all kinds of dancing, so I'm sure I'll enjoy that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, I'm sure you will.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And I'd really like to go to some concerts, if there are any.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, there are several, three performed by professionals, and one by local children.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And where is it being held?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's in the library.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Which is in Part Street, on the 18th at 6.30 in the evening.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I presume I'll need tickets for that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, you can book online.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Or you can buy them when you arrive in Kenton, either at the festival box office or from any shops displaying our logo in the windows.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, I think that'll keep me busy for the whole of my stay in Kenton.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Thank you so much for all your help.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: You're welcome.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I hope you enjoy your stay.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Goodbye.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man talking to a group of people about a tour of theatres in the city of Munich in Germany that he has arranged for them.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right, I've now almost succeeded in finalizing plans for our tour, so I'll bring you up to date with what I know.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you know, we're flying first to Munich on Monday the 4th.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The flight is at 11:30 so it's too early to have lunch at the airport.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I suggest we meet there for coffee at 10, which should give us plenty of time for breakfast before we leave home.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When we arrive in Munich, we'll be met at the airport by Claus Bauer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Claus works for a tour operator, and he'll look after us for the time we'll be in Germany.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He's already liaised with the managers of the theatres we're going to visit, and he's also arranged for an officer of the National Theatre in Munich to show us round the theater one afternoon during our stay.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now last time we discussed this trip, I didn't have the precise cost for hotel rooms, but now I have.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The normal rate at the hotel where we're staying is €150 a night for a double room.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'd hoped to get that down to 120 euros, but in fact I've been able to negotiate a rate of 110.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That'll be reflected in the final payment which you'll need to make by the end of this week.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On Tuesday, the day after our arrival, I had hoped we could sit in on a rehearsal at one of the theaters, but unfortunately that's proved very difficult to arrange.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So instead, we'll have a coach trip to one of the amazing castles in the mountains south of Munich.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On Tuesday evening, we'll all have dinner together in a restaurant near our hotel, from talking to you all about your preferences.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was clear that a typical local restaurant would be too meat oriented for some of you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some of you suggested an Italian restaurant.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But I must confess that I decided to book a Lebanese one, as we have plenty of opportunities to go to an Italian restaurant at home.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On Wednesday afternoon, the director of the play we're going to see that evening will talk to us at the theater.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: She'll describe the whole process of producing a play.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Including how she chose the actors, and as the play we're going to see is a modern one, how she worked with the playwright.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right, now I'd just like to make a few points about the plays we're going to see, partly because it might influence your choice of clothes to take with you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The play we're seeing on Wednesday evening is a modern one, and we're going to the premiere.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So it'll be quite a dressy occasion, though of course you don't have to dress formally.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I gather it's rather a multimedia production with amazing lighting effects and a soundtrack of electronic music, though unfortunately the playwright is ill and is unlikely to be able to attend.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On Thursday, we're seeing a play that was first performed last year, when it was commissioned to mark a hundred years since the birth in the town of a well-known scientist.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We're going to see a revival of that production, which aroused a lot of interest.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Friday's play will really make you think hard about what clothes to pack, as it'll be in the garden of a palace.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's a beautiful setting.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But I'd better warn you, there won't be much protection from the wind.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On Saturday, we're going by coach to a theater in another town, not far from Munich.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This will be the opening of a drama festival.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the mayor and all the other dignitaries of the town will be attending.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After the performance, the mayor is hosting a reception for all the audience.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And there'll be a band playing traditional music of the region.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After having a day off on Sunday, our final play is on Monday, and it's in the stunning setting of the Old Town Hall, which dates back to the 14th century.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The performance marks the fifty years that the lead actor has been on stage, and the play is the one where he made his first professional appearance, all those years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the day after that, we'll be flying back home.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, have you got any questions before I...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now last time we discussed this trip, I didn't have the precise cost for hotel rooms, but now I have.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The normal rate at the hotel where we're staying is €150 a night for a double room.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I'd hoped to get that down to 120 euros, but in fact I've been able to negotiate a rate of 110.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That'll be reflected in the final payment which you'll need to make by the end of this week.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: On Tuesday, the day after our arrival, I had hoped we could sit in on a rehearsal at one of the theaters, but unfortunately that's proved very difficult to arrange.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So instead, we'll have a coach trip to one of the amazing castles in the mountains south of Munich.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: On Tuesday evening, we'll all have dinner together in a restaurant near our hotel, from talking to you all about your preferences.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was clear that a typical local restaurant would be too meat oriented for some of you.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some of you suggested an Italian restaurant.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But I must confess that I decided to book a Lebanese one, as we have plenty of opportunities to go to an Italian restaurant at home.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: On Wednesday afternoon, the director of the play we're going to see that evening will talk to us at the theater.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: She'll describe the whole process of producing a play.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Including how she chose the actors, and as the play we're going to see is a modern one, how she worked with the playwright.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Right, now I'd just like to make a few points about the plays we're going to see, partly because it might influence your choice of clothes to take with you.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The play we're seeing on Wednesday evening is a modern one, and we're going to the premiere.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So it'll be quite a dressy occasion, though of course you don't have to dress formally.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I gather it's rather a multimedia production with amazing lighting effects and a soundtrack of electronic music, though unfortunately the playwright is ill and is unlikely to be able to attend.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: On Thursday, we're seeing a play that was first performed last year, when it was commissioned to mark a hundred years since the birth in the town of a well-known scientist.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We're going to see a revival of that production, which aroused a lot of interest.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Friday's play will really make you think hard about what clothes to pack, as it'll be in the garden of a palace.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's a beautiful setting.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But I'd better warn you, there won't be much protection from the wind.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: On Saturday, we're going by coach to a theater in another town, not far from Munich.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This will be the opening of a drama festival.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And the mayor and all the other dignitaries of the town will be attending.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After the performance, the mayor is hosting a reception for all the audience.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And there'll be a band playing traditional music of the region.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After having a day off on Sunday, our final play is on Monday, and it's in the stunning setting of the Old Town Hall, which dates back to the 14th century.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The performance marks the fifty years that the lead actor has been on stage, and the play is the one where he made his first professional appearance, all those years ago.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And the day after that, we'll be flying back home.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, have you got any questions before I...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: When the group meet at the airport they will have
The group will be met at Munich Airport by
options: an employee at the National Theatre.

items.title: How much will they pay per night for a double room at the hotel?
What type of restaurant will they go to on Tuesday evening?
options: a typical restaurant of the region

items.title: Who will they meet on Wednesday afternoon?

text: What does the man say about the play on each of the following days?
textReadable: What does the man say about the play on each of the following days?

list: The play was written to celebrate an anniversary.
The play will be performed inside a historic building.
The play will be accompanied by live music.
The play will be performed outdoors.
The play will be performed for the first time.
The performance will be attended by officials from the town.
items: Wednesday <input>
Thursday  <input>
Friday    <input>
Saturday  <input>
Monday    <input>
body.title: Days

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear James, who is in the Scandinavian Studies department at his university, talking to his tutor Beth.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Oh, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Uh, You must be James.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: I'm Beth Cartwright.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Please call me Beth.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Now as this is your first tutorial since you started on the Scandinavian Studies course.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: I'd like to find out something about you.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Why did you decide to take this course?
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Well, my mother is Danish, and although we always lived in England.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: She used to talk about her home a lot, and that made me want to visit Denmark.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: We hardly ever did though, my mother usually went on her own.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: But whenever her relations or friends were in England, they always came to see us.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: So I assume you already speak Danish, one of the languages you'll be studying.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: I can get by when I talk to people, though I'm not terribly accurate.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Now you probably know that you'll spend the third year of the course abroad.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Have you had any thoughts about that?
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: I'm really looking forward to it.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: And although Denmark seems the obvious place to go, because of my family connections, I'd love to spend the time in Iceland.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Oh, I'm sure it can be arranged.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Do you have any plans for when you graduate?
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: A lot of students go on to take a master's degree.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: I think the 4 years of the undergraduate course will be enough for me.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: I'm interested in journalism, and I quite like the idea of moving to Scandinavia and writing for magazines.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: I'd find that more creative than translating.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Which I suppose most graduates do.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: OK, now how are you finding the courses you're taking this term, James?
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Well, I'm really enjoying the one on Swedish cinema.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: That'll continue next term, but the one on Scandinavian literature that's running at the moment will be replaced by more specialized courses.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Oh, and by the way.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: If you're interested in watching Danish television programs, there's going to be a course on that the term after next.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: That sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Have you started thinking about the literature paper that you have to write in the next few weeks?
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Yes, my first choice would be to do something on the Icelandic sagas.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Hmm, the trouble with that is that a lot of people choose that topic, and it can be difficult to get hold of the books you'll need.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Why not leave that for another time?
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: You might find modern novels or 19th century playwrights interesting.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: I've read or seen several plays in translation, so that would be a good idea.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Fine, I'll put you down for that topic.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Right, so what would you advise me to aim at in the paper?
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: First, I suggest you avoid taking one writer and going into a great deal of detail.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: That approach certainly has its place, but I think you first need to get an understanding of the literature in the context of the society in which it was produced, who it was written for.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: How it was published and so on.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: I also think that's more fruitful than placing it within the history of the genre.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: OK, that sounds reasonable.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Could I ask for some advice about writing the paper I'm working on about the Vikings?
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: I have to do that this week, and I'm a bit stuck.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Have you decided yet what to write about?
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: No, I haven't.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: There's so much that seems interesting, Viking settlement in other countries, trade, mythology.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Well, what I suggest is that you read an assignment a student wrote last year, which is kept in the library.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: It's short and well focused, and I'm sure you'll find it helpful.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: I'll give you the details in a moment.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Textbooks usually cover so many topics, it can be very difficult to choose just one.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: I've got a DVD of the film about the Vikings that came out earlier this year.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Should I watch that again?
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: If it's the one I am thinking of... I'd ignore it.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: It's more fantasy than reality, but I've got a recording of a documentary that you should watch.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: It makes some interesting and provocative points, which I think will help you to focus your topic.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Right, so then should I work out an outline?
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Yes, just headings for different sections at this stage, and then you should start looking for suitable articles and books to draw on.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: And take notes which you organize according to those headings.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: Then put short phrases and sentences as bullet points under each heading.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: Make sure that this skeleton makes sense and flows properly before writing up the paper in full.
audioscript.speaker: BETH

audioscript.text: OK, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: That's very helpful.
audioscript.speaker: JAMES

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: James chose to take Scandinavian Studies because when he was a child
options: he was often taken to Denmark.
his mother spoke to him in Danish.
a number of Danish people visited his family.

items.title: When he graduates, James would like to
Which course will end this term?
They agree that James's literature paper this term will be on
Beth recommends that James's paper should be
options: a historical overview of the genre.
an in-depth analysis of a single writer.
a study of the social background to the literature.

body.title: How James will write his paper on the Vikings

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a lecture about conflict at work, given by a business studies lecturer.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Over the years, attitudes towards workers have changed considerably.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After all, there was a time when workers had no rights at all.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And labored in appalling conditions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Conditions have improved a lot, but conflict in the workplace is still common.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And human resources managers nowadays need to be able to deal with it when necessary.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What is conflict in the workplace?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Definitions vary, but I'm taking it to refer to a whole range of behaviors that the victim finds unacceptable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: From minor, harmless arguments to - at the opposite extreme - physical violence.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Much of this is covered by the term bullying.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By which I mean one or more people behaving abusively or aggressively against another who is in a weaker position.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Although all behavior like this is a form of conflict, not all conflict can be described in these terms.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As with all human behavior, there are numerous reasons for it, but often it's caused by someone who feels the need to show their superiority over someone else, in order to feel that they aren't at the lowest level in a hierarchy or a group of people.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In some cases, one person simply dislikes the other, on the basis that the personality of one is in some way incompatible with that of the other person.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A general habit of optimism in one person could make them intolerant of a colleague who's constantly pessimistic - not that that justifies treating them badly, of course.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some conflicts arise when people are more interested in promoting themselves and their team than in the company as a whole.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These conflicts are called 'structural', and could come about, for example, when a sales team believe they are the only people in the business who do any useful work, and look down on behind-the-scenes administrators.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Conflict obviously affects the individuals concerned - the situation is likely to be very stressful for victims, resulting in their absence from work, possibly for months.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For the company, if no effort is made to deal with conflict, it can spiral out of control, and even lead to the breakdown of the business.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some interesting work with chief executives - CEOs - has uncovered some of the reasons why they may treat colleagues badly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many CEOs combine two opposing characteristics: confidence - that is, the belief that they're capable of great achievements - with a high level of anxiety, a fear of missing targets, whether set by themselves or by the directors of the company.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This combination can make them respond badly to anyone who questions their decisions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In a high pressure work environment, such characteristics become problematic, and it's particularly difficult to tackle the situation where colleagues, managers, and board members are all trying to achieve their own visions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When they can't agree on strategic issues, and on where they see the business going, there are real problems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For managers at lower levels within the organization, it might seem that an autocratic form of management - where the chief executive gives orders and everyone else has to obey - would see more conflict than others.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Interestingly though, a company with a more democratic business model can suffer more when uncertainty about who to report to leads to conflicting demands.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now I'll say a little about dealing with the type of conflict that has harmful effects.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Of course, the ideal is to prevent it arising in the first place.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A good manager at any level will make efforts to earn the respect of the people they work with, particularly those who report to them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That will involve politeness in all communications, and treating them as equals who happen to have a different role within the organization.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Sometimes, of course, conflict does occur, and can get out of hand.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In such cases, the human resources department often gets involved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, if one of the parties in a conflict sees human resources as simply a mouthpiece for the chief executive, then an external mediator might be able to help. By talking to both sides and trying to find the truth of what's been happening.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They can build a clear picture of the situation, and give feedback that both sides will accept, precisely because they're independent.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a lecture about conflict at work, given by a business studies lecturer.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Over the years, attitudes towards workers have changed considerably.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After all, there was a time when workers had no rights at all.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And labored in appalling conditions.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Conditions have improved a lot, but conflict in the workplace is still common.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And human resources managers nowadays need to be able to deal with it when necessary.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What is conflict in the workplace?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Definitions vary, but I'm taking it to refer to a whole range of behaviors that the victim finds unacceptable.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: From minor, harmless arguments to - at the opposite extreme - physical violence.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Much of this is covered by the term bullying.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: By which I mean one or more people behaving abusively or aggressively against another who is in a weaker position.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Although all behavior like this is a form of conflict, not all conflict can be described in these terms.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As with all human behavior, there are numerous reasons for it, but often it's caused by someone who feels the need to show their superiority over someone else, in order to feel that they aren't at the lowest level in a hierarchy or a group of people.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In some cases, one person simply dislikes the other, on the basis that the personality of one is in some way incompatible with that of the other person.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A general habit of optimism in one person could make them intolerant of a colleague who's constantly pessimistic - not that that justifies treating them badly, of course.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some conflicts arise when people are more interested in promoting themselves and their team than in the company as a whole.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These conflicts are called 'structural', and could come about, for example, when a sales team believe they are the only people in the business who do any useful work, and look down on behind-the-scenes administrators.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Conflict obviously affects the individuals concerned - the situation is likely to be very stressful for victims, resulting in their absence from work, possibly for months.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For the company, if no effort is made to deal with conflict, it can spiral out of control, and even lead to the breakdown of the business.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some interesting work with chief executives - CEOs - has uncovered some of the reasons why they may treat colleagues badly.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many CEOs combine two opposing characteristics: confidence - that is, the belief that they're capable of great achievements - with a high level of anxiety, a fear of missing targets, whether set by themselves or by the directors of the company.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This combination can make them respond badly to anyone who questions their decisions.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In a high pressure work environment, such characteristics become problematic, and it's particularly difficult to tackle the situation where colleagues, managers, and board members are all trying to achieve their own visions.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When they can't agree on strategic issues, and on where they see the business going, there are real problems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For managers at lower levels within the organization, it might seem that an autocratic form of management - where the chief executive gives orders and everyone else has to obey - would see more conflict than others.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Interestingly though, a company with a more democratic business model can suffer more when uncertainty about who to report to leads to conflicting demands.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now I'll say a little about dealing with the type of conflict that has harmful effects.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Of course, the ideal is to prevent it arising in the first place.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A good manager at any level will make efforts to earn the respect of the people they work with, particularly those who report to them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That will involve politeness in all communications, and treating them as equals who happen to have a different role within the organization.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Sometimes, of course, conflict does occur, and can get out of hand.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In such cases, the human resources department often gets involved.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, if one of the parties in a conflict sees human resources as simply a mouthpiece for the chief executive, then an external mediator might be able to help. By talking to both sides and trying to find the truth of what's been happening.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They can build a clear picture of the situation, and give feedback that both sides will accept, precisely because they're independent.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

Often a result of people wanting to prove their <input>
Also caused by differences in <input> between people
'<input>' conflicts: people more concerned about own team than about company
Conflict-related stress can cause <input> that may last for months
Many have both <input> and anxiety
May not like to have their decisions questioned
There may be conflict between people who have different <input>
items.title: Chief Executives (CEOs)

items: A structure that is more <input> may create a feeling of uncertainty about who staff should report to.
items.title: Other managers

items: Bosses need to try hard to gain <input>
Someone from outside the company may be given the role of <input> in order to resolve conflicts.
items.title: Minimising conflict

body.title: Conflict at work
